memoryArtifacts.headline: Brandeston and Mountford
memoryArtifacts.description:
Brandeston and Mountford.

Radus de Brandeston Dom's de Brandeston in Com. Leicester
     |
Hugh Brandeston milles. Dom's de Brandeston & Lapwith in Com. Warw.
                       | …

[Mountford arms:] Bendy of six asure and or within a bordure gules.

Visitation of Warwickshire, 1619, p. 127

zh.lifeSummary: 當Richard de Montfort於1252年出生在英國英格蘭瓦維其可爾阿爾斯特時，他的父親Simon V de Montfort44歲，母親Eleanor of England38歲。他於大約1260年與Isabelle la Blanche of England在英國英格蘭結婚。他去世於1281年在法國崔洛易特蒙塔吉斯享年29歲。

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Montfort
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: French and Breton: variant of   Monfort   1. This surname is most common in Brittany and Haute-Savoie. See also 2 below.
Altered form of Dutch Montfoort: habitational name from Montfoort in Utrecht. It is a cognate of 1 above (see Monfort 1) and also its altered form (or perhaps vice versa).
English (of Norman origin): very rare variant of   Mountford  , a cognate of 1 above.

variants.name: Monfort
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French/Haitian Jacques, Marcel, Monique, Wilner.

French and Breton: habitational name from any of numerous places called Montfort, from Old French mont ‘hill’ (see Mont 1) + fort ‘strong, impregnable’ (from Latin fortis). This surname is by far most common in Brittany. It is also found in Haiti. Compare Montfort 1, see also 2 below.
Altered form of Dutch Montfoort, a variant of Montfort 2 and also a cognate of the French name in 1 above and an altered form of its variant Montfort 1 (or perhaps vice versa).

variants.name: Monforte
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Angelo, Salvatore, Carmela, Carmine, Rocco, Rosaria, Sal.  Spanish Carlos, Francisco, Luis, Medardo.

Italian and Spanish: habitational name from any of numerous places called Monforte, from Latin mons ‘mountain’ (genitive montis) + fortis ‘strong’, probably a reference to the strategic position of these locations.
English (of Norman origin): variant of   Montfort  . This form of the surname is rare in Britain.

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rikert
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rickert
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

en.lifeSummary: When Richard de Montfort was born in 1252, in Alcester, Warwickshire, England, United Kingdom, his father, Simon V de Montfort, was 44 and his mother, Eleanor of England, was 38. He married Isabelle la Blanche of England about 1260, in England, United Kingdom. He died in 1281, in Montargis, Loiret, Centre, France, at the age of 29.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Richard de Montfort est né en 1252, à Alcester, Warwickshire, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, son père, Simon V de Montfort, avait 44 ans et sa mère, Eleanor of England, avait 38 ans. Il a épousé Isabelle la Blanche of England environ 1260, à Angleterre, Royaume-Uni. Il est décédé en 1281, à Montargis, Loiret, Centre, France, à l’âge de 29 ans.

ko.lifeSummary: Richard de Montfort(이)가 1252년 영국 잉글랜드 워윅실르 알스스터에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Simon V de Montfort(은)는 44세였고, 그의 어머니 Eleanor of England(은)는 38세였다. 그는 대략 1260년 영국 잉글랜드에서 Isabelle la Blanche of England(와)과 결혼했다. 그는 1281년 프랑스 세트르 로이렛 몽아르지스에서 29세의 나이로 사망했다.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Richard de Montfort родился в 1252 г. в местности алсестир, варвиккшир, Англия, Великобритания, его отцу по имени Simon V de Montfort было 44 года, а матери, которую звали Eleanor of England, 38. Он женился на женщине по имени Isabelle la Blanche of England около 1260 г. в местности Англия, Великобритания. Он умер в 1281 г. в местности монтаргис, лоирет, кентр, Франция в возрасте 29 лет.
